The one The
thing that's top of mind for me, or was before this weekend, was backfilling.
It's very hard to predict what the load is going to be and where it's going to hurt.
So the best we can do right now is make sure we have the right levers to pull and the right knobs to dial when we do launch.
And so what we're doing for that is we're splitting the backfill into its own worker.

So things like processing webhooks, Ahoy events, and all the rest of the background stuff that happens for operations of the app happens in one worker, and then backfilling happens on its own worker with its own queues.
And then what that allows us to do is scale that up and down.
If the database is being hit enough that it might go down, we can either upgrade the database or scale down the workers.
these numbers.
Nice.
Scaling down the workers means people will have to wait more for the backfilling to complete.
So it's like a bit of a give or take situation.
It's not a big deal.
Like if you're putting backfill like seven days and just to have no blank data, that's enough.
then the backfills.

That's exactly what we're doing.
When you onboard, we backfill the first seven days, the latest seven days in a critical queue.
And all the rest goes in a low priority queue.
And then Stevon is working on a banner that we will show.
If the backfilling of the, not this last seven days, because you wouldn't be even let into the app if we didn't do the seven days.
So the seven days complete, you're let into the app.
And if you immediately expand the date range in a chart for some reason, you'll see the banner essentially, which is like.
Here we're backfilling.
I don't think that's going to be a big deal.
Things to watch out for are those backfills taking too long.
Even the seven-day backfills taking too long, I think that is not very likely because, again, we're limiting those to the priority queue.
Even if we get a bunch of signups, they're all processing seven days per workspace, so even large accounts in the business plan don't delay other accounts.
But if that starts taking too long, then the thing to be done there is to increase the number of workers.
Second thing to watch out for is replication lag.
If we increase the number of workers, we're increasing the number of writes that we do to the database, and we have a read-write split, so they need to be replicated.
If that starts being a problem, then we need to decrease the number of workers.
And I think those are the two.
two things that could go wrong.
I don't know if we need to go viral for this to be a problem, but a steady low number of signups that aren't concurrent won't be a problem, I don't think.
